bull_thesis:
Ferrari is a rare luxury compounder whose hard financials back up the story almost point for point. Revenue grew from 4.27 billion euros in 2021 to 7.15 billion in 2025, net income from 831 million to 1.60 billion, and earnings per share (EPS, profit divided by shares outstanding) from 4.50 to 8.97 — a five-year EPS compound annual growth rate of roughly 18.8%. The standout result for 2025 was 7.0% revenue growth on flat unit deliveries, the clearest possible proof that Ferrari's 'value over volume' model works: the company earns more from each car, not by selling more cars. Margins are high and stable — gross margin 51.7%, net margin 22.4%, free-cash-flow (FCF) margin 26.1% — and return on equity (ROE, how much profit the company generates for each euro of shareholders' money) is 39.7%. The balance sheet is net cash (cash on hand exceeds debt by roughly 1.04 billion euros), and the Altman Z-score — a widely used measure of financial distress — stands at 7.5, well above the danger zone. There is no meaningful solvency or refinancing risk even in a higher-interest-rate environment.

The execution record is exceptional. The screener shows a 100% earnings beat rate across the seven quarters it tracked. Ferrari delivered its prior business plan's 2025 financial targets — and completed a share buyback — a full year ahead of schedule, and it hit the 2026 profitability target in 2025 as well. Free cash flow has inflected sharply: industrial FCF crossed one billion euros in 2024, then 1.5 billion in 2025, and the trailing-twelve-month FCF figure is 1.62 billion, up 98.8% year over year. Capital expenditure appears to have peaked (roughly 990 million euros in 2024, guided to 900-950 million going forward), so cash conversion is improving as the investment in the new electric-vehicle manufacturing building crests.

Pricing power here rests on a sound mechanism rather than just assertion. Management explained it this way: 'We have not increased the price of the same object, but we put innovation in it.' The order book has been extended every year — now stretching toward the end of 2027 — and the main volume models are described as substantially sold out.

Management credibility is high. The same chief executive and chief financial officer have spoken on every call across eight consecutive quarters, with a consistent message and a clear sandbagging culture ('We look at the 2030 target as a floor of our ambitions'). The 2030 Capital Markets Day floor targets — 9 billion euros in revenue, 40% EBITDA margin, 30% EBIT margin — imply only roughly 4.7% annual revenue growth from a base that just delivered 7%, a deliberately conservative bar.

The near-term catalyst is the Ferrari Luce world premiere on 25 May 2026 in Rome, with deliveries beginning October 2026. The reveal — at the Transamerica building in San Francisco, designed with Jony Ive's LoveFrom studio — repositions Ferrari's first fully electric car as a halo luxury collectible rather than a volume electrification bet, consistent with the company's decision to reduce its 2030 electric-vehicle share from 40% to 20%.

Using a Buffett owner-earnings capitalisation method, the implied fair value is 567 euros per share, suggesting a margin of safety (the gap between price and estimated intrinsic value) of roughly 104% at the current price of 300.40. A Graham-formula check adds another 24%. For this picture to hold, three conditions must remain intact: luxury discretionary spending stays resilient through a late-cycle market environment; the personalisation option — which accounts for roughly 20% of car revenue — holds its appeal; and residual values (second-hand auction prices) remain strong, as they were at year-end 2025.
bear_thesis:
The 'deep value' label is a category error the transcripts themselves refute. A deep-value opportunity requires the market to be pricing terminal decline or a near-permanent earnings problem into a stock — and the market is doing no such thing here. Wall Street is not wrong about Ferrari; it correctly sees a premium compounder and prices it accordingly. The apparent 104% margin of safety from the Buffett owner-earnings method is an artefact of that single technique. Four of the six valuation models in the screener point the other way: the discounted-cash-flow (DCF) model — which estimates intrinsic value by projecting future cash flows and discounting them back to today — gives a fair value of 152.80 euros, roughly 50% below the current price; the excess profits value (EPV) and an owner-earnings approach each show the stock at zero or negative margin of safety; and only the Graham formula and the Buffett capitalisation are positive. At 26.6 times trailing-twelve-month free cash flow, with net margins 1.68 standard deviations above Ferrari's own historical peak, the bull case does not merely require the business to keep growing — it requires peak-of-cycle profitability to be sustained indefinitely rather than mean-reverting. The asymmetry runs the wrong way for a value mandate.

The growth story also took a quiet step backward. The 2030 battery-electric vehicle target was cut in half — from 40% of the model lineup to 20% — at the October 2025 Capital Markets Day, because electric demand was 'more gradual than anticipated.' Management reframed this as adaptability, but the long-range strategic plan is demonstrably softer than the iron-clad near-term financial guide, and the first electric car, the Luce, carries an undisclosed margin profile that management has declined to quantify, saying only 'you can make up your own model.' The analogy management drew to the Purosangue — which was widely understood to carry lower margins than the sports-car range — implies the Luce is a margin headwind.

The most persistently pressed topic across all eight transcript quarters is residual values (the prices second-hand Ferraris fetch at auction). Management concedes specific soft spots — the U.K. is 'a little bit under pressure,' select U.S. models have required 'commercial actions' (reducing allocation to support prices), and China deliveries have been declining every quarter since mid-2023. The top margin lever, personalisation, is deliberately self-capped: management acknowledges that adding too many personalisation options would erode the exclusivity that justifies the premium, and the personalisation mix is actually guided down slightly (from 20% to 19%) over the plan period. Meanwhile the cost base is rising: the Formula 1 budget cap is up roughly 60% (from about 135 million to 215 million dollars, flowing directly into costs), two flagship retail stores are opening in 2026 (London and New York), and the electric vehicle launch adds spending that management described as 'SG&A a negative' for the year.

The regime context makes this particularly awkward for a value framing. This is a late-cycle, fully invested market (Bank of America's Fund Manager Survey cash level is at 3.9%, a historically contrarian sell signal; net equity overweights are at 50%) with a live inflation and energy tail (oil near 92 dollars per barrel on Iran/Strait of Hormuz tensions, consumer price inflation re-accelerated to 3.8%, and the Federal Reserve on hold with no rate cut priced before roughly December). A high-ticket globally discretionary name at peak margins and 26 times free cash flow is exactly the kind of position that gets sold first in a shock. The thesis hinges on one single shared risk factor — sustained luxury-discretionary demand — which fails the portfolio-construction test of resolution-driver independence. What would confirm the bear case: a guidance reduction on personalisation mix or residual values, a soft market reception for the Luce, a disclosed electric-vehicle margin headwind in 2026, or a broad risk-off episode that re-rates the 26x multiple toward the DCF or owner-earnings range.
consensus_delta: Where we disagree with the market: for a stock labelled deep value, the operative question is whether the street is mistakenly pricing terminal decline into stable or growing earnings power. It is not — and that absence is the verdict. Wall Street is correctly constructive on Ferrari: consensus forward EPS growth is 10.3%, the company has beaten estimates in all seven tracked quarters, hit its plan a year early, and extended its order book into 2027. There is no incorrect bearish assumption to exploit; the street and the company agree the franchise compounds. The genuine analytical disagreement is internal to the valuation models, not between the market and reality. Buffett owner-earnings capitalisation yields a fair value of 567 euros (104% upside), but DCF gives 152.80 euros (roughly 50% downside), while the EPV and owner-earnings approaches each show further downside, and only the Graham formula offers a modest 24% upside. The 104% margin of safety is therefore a single-method artifact, not a crowd pricing error. If anything, the street has not fully reflected the fact that the 2030 electric-vehicle ambition was halved and that the Luce carries an undisclosed, likely dilutive, margin profile. The gap between what the market knows and what is true — the kind of disagreement that generates alpha — is effectively zero to slightly negative: the narrative is saturated, the brand is universally admired, and the mispricing that a value buyer needs does not exist.
forcing_function: The catalyst that forces the re-rating: the one hard-dated event is the Ferrari Luce world premiere on 25 May 2026 in Rome, with deliveries beginning October 2026 — the date chosen to mark the anniversary of the 1947 Ferrari 125 S race victory. Under the current regime framework this is a hard, contractual catalyst inside a broadly favourable window, which earns mild credit. But it is a product launch, not a structural special-situation with a realisation date (no spinoff, divestiture clock, restructuring milestone, or merger-arbitrage closing). Management has positioned it as a brand halo event with an undisclosed, likely dilutive margin profile, rather than a volume or product-mix re-rating. The catalyst probability score is 0.5, reflecting that it is as likely to be a 'sell the news' or margin-scare event as a positive re-rating. The other dated markers — the Q1 2026 interior reveal (which already passed in San Francisco in February 2026), the October deliveries, and the quarterly guidance cadence — are confirmatory, not consensus-breaking. Critically, none of these is the kind of forcing function a deep-value thesis demands: a buyback announcement, an activist filing, an asset sale, or a dividend initiation that compels the market to reprice a franchise it has been ignoring. The market is not ignoring this franchise. The forcing function exists on the calendar but does not force the re-rating the thesis would require — it is more likely to test the peak-margin assumption than to vindicate a hidden value.
valley_of_death:
The risky window: there is no balance-sheet danger here — net cash of roughly 1.04 billion euros, an Altman Z-score of 7.5, no debt maturity wall, no cash-burn phase; this is one of the financially safest companies in the screen. The real near-term hazards (over a three-to-nine-month horizon) are to the valuation multiple and the profit margin, not to solvency.

The first hazard is the macro regime. This is a late-cycle, fully invested, complacent market (Bank of America's Fund Manager Survey cash level at 3.9% — a historically contrarian sell signal; 50% net equity overweight; the Magnificent Seven mega-cap technology names accounting for roughly 30% of the index) sitting on a live oil and inflation tail: oil near 92 dollars per barrel on the Iran/Hormuz supply shock, consumer price inflation re-accelerated to 3.8%, and the Federal Reserve on hold with no rate cut priced before roughly December. A high-ticket globally discretionary cyclical at 26.6 times free cash flow with margins 1.68 standard deviations above its own historical peak is a prime candidate for rapid position reduction if the oil or geopolitical tripwire re-escalates and the CBOE Volatility Index (VIX) re-spikes — the April precedent saw it jump from roughly 0 to 26 in days.

The second hazard is the cost and margin step-up that management itself flagged: the Formula 1 budget cap is rising roughly 60% (from about 135 million to 215 million dollars, hitting cost directly), two flagship stores are opening in 2026 (London in the first half, New York in the second), the new paint facility is coming online, and the Luce electric vehicle is launching with an undisclosed margin profile — all while management guided that selling, general, and administrative expenses (SG&A) will be 'a negative' for the year.

The third hazard is the recurring set of soft spots: the U.K. residual-value pressure, the U.S. models requiring allocation cuts, the multi-year China delivery decline, and the personalisation ratio guided down from 20% to 19%. None of these alone destroys free cash flow, but any one can compress the peak-margin assumption that a 26x multiple is underwriting. At this valuation, multiple compression toward the DCF or owner-earnings range (roughly 150-280 euros) is the dominant near-term downside risk, and it arrives before any underlying value would be 'realised.'
positioning_washout: Forced-selling pressure: there is no mechanical washout setup here, and its absence is itself a bearish signal for an event-driven thesis. Ferrari pays a dividend but is not a yield vehicle, and there is no dividend cut, strategic break, or governance event that would force the shareholder base to sell mechanically — the buyback has already been completed (a year early), not suspended. The register is long-only quality and compounder capital (smart-money score 10 out of 100, institutional-flow score 50 out of 100, no activist 13D filing, no special-situations holders) — precisely the patient base that does not capitulate on schedule and provides no forced-seller entry point. The only mechanical flow vectors are generic and not stock-specific: broad index and ETF selling in a risk-off shock (Ferrari as a high-beta luxury constituent) and the twice-yearly Russell index reconstitution. Neither creates a thesis-specific washout that a buyer can wait for and exploit. Because there is no yield-driven base to flush and no governance event to force rotation, the 'buy the capitulation' option that would justify placing this on a watchlist for a better entry does not exist here — a buyer would simply pay full price for quality with no structural discount catalyst.
moderator_conclusion:
Operationally Ferrari is flawless. Interrogator credibility is 5 out of 5; trajectory is strengthening; the company delivered 7 out of 7 earnings beats, cleared its plan and completed its buyback a full year early, grew free cash flow through 1.5 billion euros, holds net cash, has an order book running into 2027, and can justify its pricing power with a specific mechanism rather than a brand assertion. If the committee question were simply 'is this a great business,' the answer would be an emphatic yes.

But the committee question is timing, narrative saturation, and margin of safety — where we disagree with the market — and on that axis the alpha is absent. The 104% margin of safety is a single-method artifact (Buffett owner-earnings capitalisation on a high-return-on-equity franchise) contradicted by the DCF (roughly minus 50%), the excess-profits-value model (minus 100%), and a separate owner-earnings method (minus 60%). The real picture is a compounder trading at 26.6 times free cash flow with margins 1.68 standard deviations above its own historical peak, universally covered by sell-side analysts, universally admired, with no mispricing, no activist, no forced seller, and a growth pillar — battery-electric vehicles — that was just halved from 40% to 20% of the 2030 model mix.

Applying the current regime framework moves the verdict decisively, not marginally. The framework rewards structural special situations in underfollowed, thin-coverage situations — distressed credits, spinoffs, forced sellers — and explicitly deprioritises crowded, fully priced names. Ferrari sits in the most widely covered, richest-valued lane imaginable with no special-situation angle. The regime also demands that the key risks in a thesis be independent of each other; this thesis depends entirely on sustained luxury-discretionary spending AND peak-margin persistence AND a well-received electric-vehicle launch, all simultaneously, in a late-cycle, fully invested market (Bank of America Fund Manager Survey cash at 3.9%, mega-cap technology at roughly 30% of the index) with a live oil and inflation tail (oil near 92 dollars, consumer price inflation at 3.8%, no Federal Reserve rate cut priced before roughly December) — exactly the profile to avoid mid-event.

The lone hard catalyst, the Luce premiere on 25 May 2026, is a product launch with an undisclosed, likely dilutive margin — not the buyback, activist filing, or asset-sale forcing function that a deep-value thesis requires to re-rate a mispriced franchise. And because there is no yield-driven shareholder base to flush, even the 'wait for capitulation' option is unavailable — a buyer would simply pay full price for quality.

Verdict: pass for now. A superb business but a saturated narrative at a full valuation with no structural catalyst and asymmetry running the wrong way for a value mandate. Conviction 2 out of 5: hold-quality in principle, but as an alpha proposition the edge is absent and the regime's risk-off tail argues for patience over entry. The honest framing for the portfolio director: if you own this as a high-quality compounder near full price, that is defensible — but do not underwrite it as a deep-value re-rating, because the discount the Buffett model implies is smaller than the downside if peak margins normalise or a risk-off episode hits the 26x multiple.
interrogator_dossier:
Forensic Dossier — Ferrari N.V. (RACE.MI)
Coverage: 8 consecutive quarters, Q1 2024 to FY 2025 (reported 2026-02-10). Signal type (screener): deep value. Sector: Consumer Cyclical.

1. Narrative Arc

Across these eight quarters the core story is strikingly stable in substance and evolves only at the margin — which is itself the most important observation for a 'deep value' label on a name that has compounded EPS at roughly 19% for five years.

The opening frame (Q1-Q3 2024): 'value over volume.' Every call opens on the same triad — racing, sports cars, and lifestyle — the 'three souls, one Ferrari' ethos. The growth engine explicitly disclosed is product mix plus personalisation, not unit volume: Q1 2024 showed 'double-digit growth on key metrics with flat deliveries'; Q2 2024 saw revenues rise 16%, 'sustained by the continuing strength of the product mix and the growing demand for personalisation.' The forward-looking pillar introduced in 2024 is electrification and infrastructure — the new electric-vehicle manufacturing building (inaugurated June 2024, exactly two years from its announcement), in-house battery modules, electric axles, and inverters (the F80 as proof), and the promise of the first fully electric Ferrari.

The mid-arc expansion (FY 2024 to Q1 2025): the story adds a hard-dated catalyst calendar. The FY 2024 call (February 2025) set 'October 9' as a fixed event — 'the launch of the Ferrari full electric.' Q1 2025 formalised a three-step reveal (technology heart at the October Capital Markets Day, interior in early 2026, world premiere in spring 2026, deliveries October 2026). Six model launches were promised for 2025; 'quality of revenues over quantity' was repeated verbatim.

The pivot quietly arrives (Q3 2025, the October 9 Capital Markets Day): this is the single most important narrative event in the window. Management recalibrated the 2030 powertrain offer from 20% internal-combustion engine / 40% hybrid / 40% electric (the 2022 plan) to 40% internal-combustion / 40% hybrid / 20% electric. The full-electric ambition that anchored the 2024 forward story was halved. Management framed this not as a retreat but as 'electrification as an addition, not a transition,' reusing the flexible manufacturing-building narrative to absorb a 20-point cut to the battery-electric vehicle mix. Simultaneously the Capital Markets Day set a 2030 floor (9 billion euros revenue, 40% EBITDA, 30% EBIT) and the company confirmed it had hit its old 2025 targets — and completed the buyback — one year early.

Current frame (FY 2025, February 2026): the battery-electric vehicle is now named 'Ferrari Luce,' revealed in San Francisco (Transamerica building, LoveFrom — Jony Ive's design studio), world premiere fixed for 25 May 2026, deliveries October 2026. 2025 closed at record financials (revenue above 7.1 billion euros, EBIT above 2.1 billion, industrial free cash flow above 1.5 billion), all 2025 targets and the buyback achieved a year ahead. The forward emphasis has rotated subtly from 'electrification leadership' toward innovation breadth as the pricing-power justification — vehicle dynamics, user interface, 'humanism of technology,' the reintroduction of physical steering-wheel buttons — signalling that management is pre-empting questions about whether the price and mix lever still has room to run.

In summary: the thesis did not contract — revenue and margin ambition rose at the Capital Markets Day. What contracted is the electric leg of the story, repackaged as optionality. Growth drivers introduced and maintained: personalisation, horizontal model diversification (roughly four new models per year), and lifestyle and flagship retail (London and New York stores in 2026). The driver quietly de-emphasised: battery-electric vehicles as a volume and mix driver.

2. Claims Versus Financials (Forensic)

'Double-digit growth on key metrics / record results' (Q1-FY 2024): revenue grew from 5.97 billion to 6.68 billion euros (up 12% in FY 2024); net income from 1.25 billion to 1.52 billion (up 22%); EPS from 6.90 to 8.46 (up 22.6%). Verdict: verified. FY 2024 EPS growth even exceeded the 'double-digit' framing.

'Value over volume — growth from mix and personalisation, not units' (every call): FY 2024 shipments rose only 89 units (roughly 0.6%), yet revenue rose 12%; FY 2025 revenue rose 7.0% on flat deliveries. Verdict: fully verified. This is the cleanest claim-to-number match in the file. Pricing and mix, not volume, is doing the work.

'Industrial free cash flow surpassing 1 billion euros for the first time (FY 2024); above 1.5 billion (FY 2025)' (FY 2024, FY 2025): the screener shows FCF margin of 26.1%, FCF up 98.8% year over year, and trailing-twelve-month FCF of 1.62 billion. Verdict: verified and then some. The FCF growth is the standout data point and aligns with the 'record cash generation' claim.

'Beat cadence and consistent delivery' (all quarters): the screener shows 7 beats from 7 quarters. Verdict: verified. Zero misses across the window.

'Margins strengthening on mix despite inflation and brand spending' (FY 2024, FY 2025): gross margin is 51.7% (stable); net margin is 22.4%; EBIT margin rose to 28.3% in FY 2024, and the Capital Markets Day floor is 30% EBIT / 40% EBITDA. Verdict: verified, with the caveat that margin is 1.68 standard deviations above Ferrari's own historical peak — the level is real but extended, creating an expectations risk rather than a credibility problem.

'2030 floor: 9 billion euros revenue, 40% EBITDA, 30% EBIT' (Q3 2025 Capital Markets Day): the 2025 base is 7.15 billion euros revenue, with EBIT already at the level the old 2026 target had set — a year early. The implied growth from 2025 to 2030 is roughly 26% over five years, or approximately 4.7% per year, from a base that just grew 7%. Verdict: plausible and internally consistent — a deliberately conservative bar ('floor'), consistent with the structural sandbagging pattern.

'Order book covers well into 2026 and extends toward end of 2027' (progressively across calls): not directly in financials, but consensus forward EPS growth of 10.3% and the 7% reported revenue support the visibility claim. Verdict: credible. The order-book claim has been directionally extended each year without being walked back.

'Battery-electric vehicle and electrification leadership' (2024 calls): no electric-vehicle revenue yet; the 2030 battery-electric vehicle share was cut from 40% to 20% at the Capital Markets Day. Verdict: the one claim that diverged materially from its own trajectory. The 2024 narrative implied a faster ramp than the 2025 plan now embeds.

Where the story is richer than the numbers justify: the emotional and brand prose (Le Mans, museum imagery, 'the elephant into a horse') vastly outweighs its income-statement footprint; lifestyle and racing remain small, structurally dilutive contributors (management concedes that non-car revenue is 'dilutive to profitability,' Q1 2025). Where the story is more conservative than the trajectory: near-term guidance. The company has repeatedly hit numbers 'one year early,' meaning reported results have run ahead of the guided path — the narrative undersells the delivery.

The deep-value tension the financials expose: the screener carries a Buffett fair value of 567 euros (104% margin of safety) alongside a DCF fair value of 152.80 euros (roughly minus 50%), with a wide per-method spread — Graham at plus 24%, but DCF at minus 97%, excess-profits value at minus 100%, a separate owner-earnings method at minus 60%, and an intrinsic-value-15 method at minus 8%. The transcripts do not resolve this; if anything they confirm the bear side of the model disagreement: this is a 26.6x-free-cash-flow, 1.68-standard-deviation-peak-margin compounder, not a mispriced asset the transcripts reveal as hidden value. The 'deep value' classification is an artifact of one valuation method applied to a high-return-on-equity franchise, not a market dislocation the calls reveal.

3. Tone and Confidence Trajectory

Rating: stable to modestly rising, with a deliberate layer of defensive hedging added from Q1 2025 onward.

Q1 2024: offensive and celebratory — 'firing on all cylinders, charging ahead.' Pure growth and investment language.
Q2 2024: confident, guidance-raising — 'we upgraded our 2024 guidance.' Peak offensive tone in the window.
Q3 2024: confident but with the first macro caveat — 'we are conscious of the macro environment around us and we continue to monitor it very carefully.' The 'four wheels on the ground' hedge appears here.
FY 2024: offensive again on results and forward-looking — 'even better positioned compared to our initial trajectory.'
Q1 2025: defensive overlay appears — 'while the macroeconomic environment remains uncertain with ongoing geopolitical tensions and market volatility.' Notably adds: 'Q1 2025 results were not impacted in any way by the recent introduction of higher tariffs.'
Q2 2025: most defensive of the window — 'we remain confident and well prepared to navigate potential macro threats, including trade tensions, currency fluctuation and financial market volatility, which require an increased level of cautiousness; we cannot say that we are completely immune.'
Q3 2025 (Capital Markets Day): re-offensive but recalibrating — sets the 9 billion / 40% / 30% floor and the 'look at 2030 as a floor of our ambitions' framing, while cutting the battery-electric vehicle mix. Confidence is high but the content is a downgrade of one pillar.
FY 2025: triumphant on delivery, disciplined on outlook — 'remarkable year, all financial targets achieved one year ahead,' balanced against 'the discipline that is required in the current context.'

Specificity over time is increasing on financials and the catalyst calendar (exact dates: October 9 Capital Markets Day, 25 May 2026 premiere, October 2026 deliveries) but deliberately decreasing on the two most-probed items — the magnitude of guidance beats ('I don't want to go into the details of how much greater than,' Q2 2025) and the Luce volumes and pricing ('you can make up your own model,' FY 2025). The shift from offensive to partially defensive language is real but is macro-driven (tariffs, foreign exchange, geopolitics), not a sign of deteriorating business confidence.

4. Guidance Credibility

Reliability: high.

The forensic test — earlier guidance versus later delivered results — is unusually clean here. The FY 2024 guidance was upgraded mid-year (Q2 2024) and then delivered or exceeded. The previous business plan's 2025 financial targets and buyback were achieved a full year early (confirmed at Q3 2025 and FY 2025), and the 2026 profitability target from the old plan was hit in 2025 — 'our revised guidance exceeds the profitability target we had originally set for 2026, one year in advance.' The screener's 7-from-7 beat rate corroborates this across every tracked quarter.

Structural sandbagging: yes, and it appears deliberate. Three independent tells: (1) hitting plan a year early, twice; (2) the chief financial officer's refusal to quantify 'greater than' while the implied result was 'close to the guidance floor' (Q2 2025) — a classic under-promise; (3) framing the entire 2030 Capital Markets Day plan as a 'floor of our ambitions.' When asked directly whether the free-cash-flow guide was conservative given that half the year's FCF was already generated in Q1, the chief financial officer replied 'we are never conservative; in principle, there might be chances in that direction' (Q1 2025) — a denial that functions as confirmation.

The one credibility note: the 2022-to-2025 powertrain recalibration (battery-electric vehicle share from 40% to 20%) means the 2022 Capital Markets Day product-mix guidance was not delivered as framed. Management handled this transparently and reframed it as adaptability, but a director must register that the long-dated strategic guide (eight years out) is softer than the one-year financial guide (which has been reliable). They sandbag the near term and flex the far term.

5. Analyst Pressure Points

The same analysts — Tom Narayan at RBC, Adam Jonas at Morgan Stanley, Thomas Besson at Kepler, Jose Asumendi at JPMorgan, and Michael Tyndall at HSBC — return to a tight set of recurring probes.

Residual values are the single most-pressed topic across all eight quarters (FY 2024: 'fake news blog'; Q2 2025: 'is this impacting the stock price right now?'). Management consistently concedes specific soft spots — the United Kingdom 'a little bit under pressure' and certain United States models requiring 'commercial actions' (reducing allocation) — while insisting there is no systemic problem. This is a real, recurring soft underbelly that management contains but never fully dismisses. The risk of over-personalisation hurting second-hand values is an acknowledged structural concern.

The personalisation ceiling is pressed repeatedly ('is there peak carbon fibre?' — Berenberg, FY 2025). Management's answer is telling: they deliberately cap certain personalisation options 'otherwise we will not be personalised or special anymore.' This means the highest-margin revenue lever is self-limited by the exclusivity model — there is a structural ceiling on the very driver most responsible for the margin level.

China weakness: deliveries have been 'decreasing since Q2 2023, quarter after quarter' (noted by Equita at Q1 2025). Management deflects to an '8-10% of range' target and attributes the decline to local tax treatment of twelve-cylinder cars; no clean China growth answer has been given.

Luce and Elettrica volumes, pricing, and margin drag are probed on every recent call. Management systematically declines to quantify, deferring with 'make up your own model' (FY 2025) and the Purosangue analogy ('we told you everything when the shape was visible'). The margin impact of the battery-electric vehicle versus high-volume range models is the open question management will not answer.

Tariffs and foreign exchange have been pressed since Q1 2025. Management notes that the first and second quarters of 2025 were shielded by existing United States inventory and a 90-day repricing right, and that it took 'up to 10%' in United States price action, but conceded the inventory shield is temporary.

Margin stability versus investment load: Jose Asumendi at the Q3 2025 Capital Markets Day observed that the market may have doubts about margin stability given the Elettrica launch, the new paint facility, and the Formula 1 budget-cap increase (from roughly 135 million to 215 million dollars). Management answered philosophically — 'innovation equals sustainability' — rather than with a financial bridge, a non-answer to a quantitative question.

The pattern: deflections cluster on (a) battery-electric vehicle economics and (b) residual-value durability — the two things that would most damage the bull case if they turned negative. None has done so yet, but the consistency of the non-answers is the yellow flag.

6. Red Flags and Green Flags

Green flags:

100% beat rate (7 from 7 quarters) plus targets hit a year early, twice — elite execution and a conservative guidance culture.

Free-cash-flow inflection — industrial FCF crossed 1 billion euros in FY 2024, then 1.5 billion in FY 2025; the screener shows FCF up 98.8% year over year. Capital expenditure appears to have peaked (roughly 990 million euros in FY 2024, guided 900-950 million) so cash conversion is improving as the electric-vehicle manufacturing investment crests.

Order book extended, not contracted — visibility pushed from 'into 2026' to 'toward end of 2027' across the window; range models 'substantially sold out.'

Pricing power validated by mechanism rather than assertion (Q3 2025): management's defence — 'we have not increased the price of the same object, but we put innovation in it' — is the correct and durable form of pricing power.

Balance sheet: net cash of roughly 1.04 billion euros (cash exceeds debt), Altman Z-score of 7.5. Zero solvency risk.

Residual values 'stable and solid; recent auctions achieved strong valuations' (FY 2025) — the most-pressed risk showed improvement by year-end.

Red flags:

Battery-electric vehicle ambition cut in half (from 40% to 20% of the 2030 lineup, Q3 2025 Capital Markets Day) — the forward growth pillar of the 2024 narrative was materially downgraded; demand for electric vehicles was 'more gradual than anticipated.' Adaptive, but a real miss versus the 2022 promise.

Margin 1.68 standard deviations above its own historical peak (screener) — the profitability level the market capitalises is statistically extended; mean-reversion risk is asymmetric to the downside.

Personalisation is self-capped — the top margin lever has a deliberate ceiling (exclusivity protection), is only visible four to five months out, and the personalisation mix is guided down from 20% to 19% over the plan period.

Persistent regional softness — the United Kingdom and select United States residual-value and demand soft spots requiring commercial actions; China in a multi-year delivery decline.

Rising cost load — Formula 1 budget cap up roughly 60% (from about 135 million to 215 million dollars, flowing directly into costs), two flagship stores opening in 2026, new paint facility, and Elettrica launch — all hitting selling, general, and administrative expenses and research and development costs as management guides those lines 'a negative' for the year.

Luce margin drag undisclosed — management's refusal to frame battery-electric vehicle economics, combined with the analogy to the Purosangue's known margin dilution, signals that the first electric model is likely a margin headwind it is not yet ready to quantify.

7. Hidden Signals

Terminology migration on the battery-electric vehicle: 'the Ferrari full electric' (2024) became 'Ferrari Elettrica' (Q1 2025) and then 'Ferrari Luce' (FY 2025), unveiled with LoveFrom (Jony Ive's design studio) at the Transamerica building. The shift from a generic engineering descriptor to a named, designer-branded luxury object with a theatrical three-step reveal is a deliberate repositioning of the electric car from a powertrain transition to a halo luxury collectible — consistent with cutting the battery-electric vehicle mix to 20%.

'Floor' as a recurring frame (Q3 2025 onward): 'we look at the 2030 target as a floor of our ambitions' appears repeatedly. This is new vocabulary that institutionalises the sandbagging — management is pre-anchoring future beats.

Reintroduction of physical buttons and 'humanism of technology' (FY 2025): a quiet admission that the prior technology-forward touch-control direction was walked back on client feedback — a small but real 'we listen and adapt' signal that supports the client-centricity claim but also shows the product team correcting course.

Capitalised research and development rising as amortisation falls (flagged by Stifel at Q3 2024): management conceded that the capitalisation rate 'is going to grow' as spending shifts to new-product development. This is a flattering near-term accounting tailwind to reported operating profit (EBIT) that should be watched as the Elettrica and four-models-per-year cadence loads the research-and-development line.

No management turnover: the chief executive and the chief financial officer are constant across all eight quarters; investor-relations rotates but the principal voices and message discipline are unbroken. Stability of voice means high signal reliability and low 'new-CFO kitchen-sink' risk.

Capital allocation: the buyback was completed early; no dividend cut or pivot appears anywhere. The shareholder base is not facing a forced rotation. The smart-money score of 10 out of 100 and the institutional-flow score of 50 out of 100 (screener) suggest no activist or event-driven setup — this is a quality compounder held by long-only quality investors, not a special-situations target.

8. Capital-Allocation Verdict (for the portfolio director)

Classification: strengthening operationally — but the screener's 'deep value' label is a category error the transcripts refute.

Ferrari is executing at an elite level: 7 from 7 beats, targets cleared a year early, free cash flow inflecting hard, order book extending into 2027, net cash, pricing power that is mechanistically sound rather than merely asserted, and a chief-financial-officer culture of structural sandbagging ('floor,' 'never conservative'). On business trajectory this is unambiguously strengthening.

But the single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing is that this is not a value dislocation — it is a fully priced quality compounder whose apparent margin of safety is entirely an artifact of one valuation method. The Buffett owner-earnings capitalisation (fair value 567 euros, 104% upside) sits against a DCF of 152.80 euros (minus 50%), an excess-profits value of minus 100%, and a separate owner-earnings method of minus 60%; only the Graham formula (plus 24%) and the Buffett model are positive. At 26.6 times free cash flow with margins 1.68 standard deviations above the company's own historical peak, the bull case requires the franchise to sustain peak profitability and keep compounding, not revert. The transcripts give real comfort on durability — brand, order book, pricing mechanism — but offer no catalyst, no event, and no mispricing the market is missing. Smart-money score is 10 out of 100; no activist filing; no forced seller; no dividend pivot. The recalibrated battery-electric vehicle offer (the Luce) is the one place expectations were trimmed, and its margin profile remains undisclosed.

Decision rule for the director: size this as a high-quality compounder bought near full price, underwriting continued execution and pricing power — not as a deep-value re-rating. The asymmetry runs the wrong way for a value mandate: peak-of-cycle margins plus 26x free cash flow plus a halved electric-vehicle ambition means the downside on any execution wobble or margin mean-reversion is larger than the discount the Buffett model implies. The thesis is the quality of the franchise; the risk is the price you pay for it.

Management credibility 5 of 5; trajectory strengthening.
